description: Intersection: a large truck approaches laterally from the left at 8 m/s while a pedestrian stands ahead-left. Ported from legacy Test_scene/scenario_manager_test1.py; preview snapshot extrapolated 1 s backwards.

branches.rationale: Deliberation missed its deadline; anytime fallback to the minimal-risk maneuver.

matched_branch.rationale: Deliberation missed its deadline; anytime fallback to the minimal-risk maneuver.

consolidation.assessment: Full emergency braking was a poor fallback here: it did not address the lateral closing risk from the truck and still allowed a side impact. The severe instability afterward shows the maneuver was not just insufficient, it was unsafe in this intersection context. It reduced neither collision risk nor consequence in a meaningful way.
consolidation.lesson: In an intersection, if a large vehicle is crossing laterally, do not rely on straight-line hard braking alone; immediately assess lateral threat and, if space exists, combine braking with an escape path away from the crossing vehicle while maintaining stability.
consolidation.better_alternative: Controlled maximum braking with a simultaneous swerve/steer away from the truck’s path into the clearest available open lane or shoulder, if that path was verified to be free of pedestrians and barriers.

execution_outcome: Side collision with the truck occurred, followed by vehicle instability. Both vehicle damage and injuries were severe.

trace.output: scene projected 0.90s forward to the anticipated execution state (risk 0.666)

output.summary: Highest binding risk is the pedestrian: scene_risk is 0.426 with the pedestrian carrying the highest HJ risk (0.71) and occupying the left corridor, while left lane change is not possible. Truck1 is the more immediate dynamic threat, being on a collision course with TTC 2.1 s and miss distance 3.12 m, but its combined risk is lower than the pedestrian’s. Braking alone is sufficient at execution, and the right corridor remains open.

Intersection scene with ego moving at 10 m/s and two actors on the left side of the ego path. A stationary pedestrian is 16 m ahead and 4 m left, close to the forward-left corridor and the highest immediate conflict. A large truck is farther ahead-left at 23 m and 14.4 m left, moving laterally toward the ego/right side, but it remains well outside the near-left evasive space. Right side appears clear in the observed geometry.

hazards.note: Stationary vulnerable road user 16 m ahead, 4 m left; near forward-left corridor, high immediate collision risk if ego or avoidance path shifts left.
Large vehicle 23 m ahead, 14.4 m left, moving toward ego/right laterally; potential crossing/closing hazard but currently farther and offset from ego lane.

output.occlusions: Truck size can occlude additional traffic/pedestrians from the far-left sector near the intersection. No right-side occluder evident.

output.assessment: Full emergency braking was a poor fallback here: it did not address the lateral closing risk from the truck and still allowed a side impact. The severe instability afterward shows the maneuver was not just insufficient, it was unsafe in this intersection context. It reduced neither collision risk nor consequence in a meaningful way.
output.lesson: In an intersection, if a large vehicle is crossing laterally, do not rely on straight-line hard braking alone; immediately assess lateral threat and, if space exists, combine braking with an escape path away from the crossing vehicle while maintaining stability.
output.better_alternative: Controlled maximum braking with a simultaneous swerve/steer away from the truck’s path into the clearest available open lane or shoulder, if that path was verified to be free of pedestrians and barriers.
